svg.text: RUSSIAN
HISTORY & EAST SLAVIC ORIGINS
KIEVAN RUS — SHARED ANCESTOR
862 CE: Varangian (Viking) prince Rurik founds Rus in Novgorod
882 CE: Oleg moves capital to KYIV — both nations’ origin city
988 CE: Christianization of Kievan Rus under Vladimir the Great
Old East Slavic: ancestor of Russian, Ukrainian, AND Belarusian
1240 CE: Mongol invasion destroys Kyiv; Rus splinters
RUSSIAN LITERARY LANGUAGE
Moscow emerged as center after Mongol period (14th c.)
Russian Church Slavonic remained literary standard for centuries
1703: Peter the Great reforms Russian alphabet (civil script)
~150M native speakers; official in 4 UN member states
Russian is the 8th most spoken language in the world
UKRAINIAN HISTORY · SECONDARY LANGUAGE
UKRAINIAN LITERARY TRADITION
Ukrainian distinct from Russian by 13th century
1596: Union of Brest — Catholic/Orthodox split shapes Ukraine
1798: Ivan Kotliarevsky writes Eneida in modern Ukrainian
Taras Shevchenko (1814-1861): national poet; Kobzar collection
Shevchenko established Ukrainian as literary language
SUPPRESSION & REVIVAL
1863 Ems Decree: Russian Empire banned Ukrainian publications
1876 Ems Decree expanded: banned all Ukrainian language use
Soviet Ukrainization (1920s) then reversed by Stalinist terror
Executed Renaissance (Rozstriliane vidrodzhennya): writers killed
1991 independence: Ukrainian becomes sole official language
HOLODOMOR (1932-33)
Soviet-engineered famine killed 3.5-7.5M Ukrainians
Ukrainian farmers targeted; cultural elite executed
Recognized as genocide by 30+ countries
TWO LANGUAGES, ONE ORIGIN, SEPARATE IDENTITIES
Kyiv: founding city of Kievan Rus — claimed by BOTH nations
Russian: global power; UN language; Pushkin, Tolstoy, Dostoevsky
Ukrainian: national resistance language; Shevchenko, Franko
Linguistic difference: vocative case, /h/, vowel quality
Political difference: sovereignty, self-determination, identity
PLATE 24d ALT · RUSSIAN / UKRAINIAN · HISTORY
